antipattern: Computing an expensive elementwise reduction (log_softmax/softmax) over the full tensor and only afterwards indexing a subset of rows, instead of indexing first and reducing over the needed rows only.
match_logic: Match an assignment of F.log_softmax / F.softmax (or torch variants) applied to a full tensor (no subscript indexing before dim=), which is the hot-path form that later gets subset-indexed. The subsequent indexing (var[idx, ...]) reveals only part is needed.

antipattern: Using all_gather_object to replicate a full serialized payload onto every rank when only rank 0 consumes the result, where gather_object to rank 0 would suffice.
match_logic: Match a call to torch.distributed.all_gather_object(...) that materializes a full [None] * world_size receive buffer on all ranks; such calls are suspect when the gathered data is later gated behind a rank==0 check.

antipattern: Allocating a fresh torch.cuda.Stream() inside __init__ (or per-object construction), so N objects create N CUDA streams instead of sharing one.
match_logic: Match a call that constructs a new CUDA stream (torch.cuda.Stream()) used inline as a constructor argument or assigned per-instance, indicating per-instance allocation of an expensive shared resource.

antipattern: Allocating a tensor on CPU with torch.zeros/ones/empty and then moving it to GPU via .cuda() (or .to(device)), incurring a host allocation plus a host-to-device copy every step instead of allocating directly on device.
match_logic: Match a torch tensor-creation call (zeros/ones/empty/full) whose chained call sequence ends in .cuda() — possibly with intervening .type(...)/.to(...) — indicating CPU allocation followed by an H2D transfer.

antipattern: A fused/optimized backend kernel is gated behind a negated platform check (e.g. `not current_platform.is_cuda()`), so on that very platform the code falls through to the slow eager/native path.
match_logic: A boolean guard that enables a hardware-specific fast path but combines a backend/flash-attn flag with a *negated* platform predicate `not current_platform.is_<plat>()`, which inverts the intended dispatch.
negative_example: if not current_platform.is_cuda():  # single guard, no fast-path selection

antipattern: Deriving a scalar (e.g. max_seq_len / num_reqs) from a CPU-shadow tensor via .max().item() inside a per-step attention metadata build forces a blocking GPU->CPU sync (or relies on a lazy CPU shadow that must be kept in sync).
match_logic: Match a line inside metadata/decode build code that reduces a *_cpu / seq_lens tensor with .max() and immediately materializes it to a Python scalar via .item(), which is a per-step D2H sync point.

antipattern: Indexing a GPU tensor with a NumPy array (or CPU-derived index built via np.*) forces an implicit blocking host-to-device copy of the index array.
match_logic: Find a variable assigned from a numpy expression (np.cumsum / np.arange / np.array etc.) and later used directly as a tensor subscript index without being converted to a torch device tensor.

antipattern: Building a small metadata tensor directly on the GPU (device=self.device) that is later consumed by a CPU-synchronizing op like .item()/.tolist(), forcing a blocking device-to-host sync.
match_logic: Match a torch.tensor(...) constructor that explicitly passes device=self.device (or device=<gpu var>) — such index/seqlen metadata tensors are typically fed into .item()/.tolist() and should be built on CPU instead.

antipattern: An unconditional .cpu() call inside a generic serialization/encoder hot path forces a hidden device-to-host copy for every GPU tensor that passes through.
match_logic: Match a tensor transform chain that includes .cpu() combined with a serialization-oriented conversion such as .view(torch.uint8), .numpy(), or a contiguous byte-view flatten chain — i.e., a .cpu() inserted into the byte-serialization path rather than at an explicit CPU-copy site.
negative_example: prompt_embeds = prompt_embeds.cpu()  # explicit device transfer at input preprocessing

antipattern: All-gathering a tensor across the TP group and then immediately slicing/splitting it back down to only the local rank's shard, which throws away the just-gathered data and wastes the collective plus allocations.
match_logic: Match an all_gather-style call assigned into a variable that is subsequently indexed by the local TP rank (e.g. split_tensor_along_last_dim(...)[self.tp_rank]), indicating the gathered result is discarded except for the local shard.

antipattern: Allocating a tensor on CPU (often pinned) and immediately copying it to the device with .to(...), instead of constructing it directly on the target device.
match_logic: Match a torch tensor factory (zeros/ones/empty/full/arange/tensor) whose call chain ends in a .to(device, ...) — indicating a CPU allocation immediately transferred to device that could be constructed directly on-device.

antipattern: A pre-allocated device tensor is filled via an unconditional `.copy_(..., non_blocking=True)` from a CPU staging tensor without checking whether the target device is CPU, causing a redundant CPU→CPU copy (and duplicate allocation) when running on CPU.
match_logic: Match a `.copy_(...)` call that uses `non_blocking=True` — the non_blocking flag signals a host→device transfer pattern that is a wasted no-op copy when the destination is actually on CPU; such copies should be guarded by a device!=cpu check.

antipattern: Allocating a scratch/workspace buffer with torch.zeros when it will be fully overwritten, incurring a redundant device-wide memset on every call.
match_logic: Match assignment of a variable whose name suggests a scratch/workspace/cache/buffer to torch.zeros(...), which zero-fills a buffer that is typically overwritten; torch.empty is the cheaper alternative.

antipattern: Reassigning a local list variable to sorted(same_var, ...) allocates a new list when an in-place .sort() would suffice, wasting CPU/allocations in hot paths.
match_logic: Match an assignment where the left-hand name is re-assigned from sorted() called on that very same name, i.e. `x = sorted(x, ...)` — a self-reassign that could be `x.sort(...)`.

antipattern: Copying the return value of a function into a pre-allocated slice buffer instead of passing that buffer as an out= argument, causing an extra full device-to-device copy.
match_logic: Match a `.copy_(...)` call whose target is a sliced buffer, where the argument is a variable that was assigned from a function call (typical pattern: `x = some_func(...)` then `buf[:n].copy_(x)`). The regex flags the `buf[:...].copy_(var)` form which signals writing a freshly-computed tensor into a preallocated slice rather than computing in place via out=.

antipattern: Computing a small prefix-sum on-device via torch.cumsum(..., out=...) into a freshly torch.zeros-allocated GPU buffer, incurring an extra kernel launch and allocation per step instead of computing the tiny cumulative sum on CPU and doing a single async H2D copy.
match_logic: Match a torch.cumsum call that writes into a pre-allocated buffer via the out= argument (typically the start_loc / prefix-sum pattern), which signals device-side scalar prefix-sum work that should be done on host.

antipattern: Calling .item() on a device tensor to pass a Python scalar into a kernel/op on the per-forward hot path forces a device-to-host sync and stalls the GPU pipeline.
match_logic: Match a tensor (optionally indexed) that has .item() called on it and is passed as an argument to a function call — i.e. .item() used to produce a scalar argument rather than being stored/logged.

antipattern: Calling len() on the result of an accessor that materializes/copies a full collection (e.g. get_token_ids(), get_tokens(), to_list()) just to obtain a length, incurring an O(L) allocation instead of an O(1) length lookup.
match_logic: Match len(...) wrapping a call to a getter-style method whose name suggests it constructs/returns a fresh collection (get_*, to_list, tolist, as_list, all_*, list_*), where a cheaper get_len()/count/size likely exists.

antipattern: An async loop that unconditionally does work every iteration and only yields via asyncio.sleep(0), busy-polling the event loop and burning CPU instead of waiting on an event/condition.
match_logic: Match a call to asyncio.sleep with a zero (0 or 0.0) argument, which is the tell-tale of a busy-poll loop that yields the event loop without actually blocking on any signal.

antipattern: Splitting a fused tensor via reshape+torch.split+squeeze+.contiguous() forces redundant memory copies where a single chunk/view would suffice.
match_logic: Match a call to .squeeze(...) immediately chained with .contiguous(), which is the tell-tale of the reshape+split+squeeze+contiguous fused-tensor unpacking pattern that materializes extra buffers instead of using chunk/views.

antipattern: A pin_memory() call is executed unconditionally inside a generic offload branch without checking the dedicated pin-memory config flag, allocating page-locked host memory even when the user disabled pinning.
match_logic: Match an assignment where the RHS is a .pin_memory(...) call. The heuristic: pin_memory should almost always be guarded by an explicit *_pin_memory config flag; a bare invocation is suspicious because it forces page-locked allocation regardless of configuration.

antipattern: Moving a GPU tensor to CPU via .to('cpu')/.cpu() on a hot path forces a blocking device→host copy and CUDA synchronization.
match_logic: Match a tensor expression that is explicitly transferred to the CPU device via .to('cpu')/.to("cpu")/.to(device='cpu') or a trailing .cpu() call, which triggers a blocking D2H copy.

antipattern: Moving a GPU tensor to CPU via .cpu().numpy() to run a numpy op then copying back to device, forcing a GPU→CPU→GPU sync when a native torch op exists.
match_logic: Match a call chain that transfers a tensor to host memory as a numpy array (.cpu().numpy() or .detach().cpu().numpy()) which typically precedes a numpy computation and a copy back to device.

antipattern: Calling .item() on a per-element tensor result inside an accumulation expression (e.g. total += x.item()**n) forces a device→host sync and CPU scalar math on every iteration instead of keeping the reduction on-device.
match_logic: Match an augmented-assignment accumulator whose right-hand side calls .item() (optionally raised to a power), which turns a running tensor reduction into repeated blocking d2h syncs.

antipattern: Calling .item() on a device tensor inside (or per-iteration of) a gradient/norm accumulation, forcing a blocking device→host sync per element instead of accumulating on-device and syncing once.
match_logic: Match an in-place accumulation statement whose right-hand side calls .item() on a tensor (typically a per-parameter norm), e.g. `total_norm += param_norm.item()**2`. This is the pre-fix form where a device→host transfer happens each iteration.

antipattern: Calling .item() on a GPU tensor inside an accumulation/reduction expression forces a blocking GPU→CPU sync per iteration, serializing the device.
match_logic: Match an accumulation (e.g. `x += ...`) whose right-hand side calls `.item()`, or a scalar-math expression like `.item()**(...)` where the tensor value could stay on device.
negative_example: final_scalar = total_norm.item()  # single sync after loop

antipattern: Calling the global torch.cuda.synchronize() (full device barrier) to order work against a side stream, which serializes all compute/comm and defeats overlap; stream.wait_stream should be used instead.
match_logic: Match a call to torch.cuda.synchronize() with no device-index argument (the full-device barrier form), which is the over-synchronizing primitive that should be a per-stream wait_stream/wait_event instead.

antipattern: An early-exit "fast path" checks only a size==1 condition but omits the group/handle-is-None case, so when the collective is disabled the code falls through and does expensive allocation+copy work that is then discarded because the actual collective is separately guarded on the None check.
match_logic: Match an if-condition that short-circuits on a "size == 1" (or "world_size == 1") test without also including a "<group> is None" disjunct — i.e. an `if ... _size == 1:` guard that lacks an accompanying `is None` check for the parallel group.
negative_example: if mp_group is None or mp_size == 1:

antipattern: Constructing a tensor from a Python list literal with a non-CPU device forces a CPU materialization plus a host-to-device copy on every call; use torch.zeros/ones/full/empty directly on the device instead.
match_logic: Match torch.tensor(...) whose first argument is a Python list literal and whose kwargs include a device= argument (i.e. building a small constant tensor from a list targeting a specific device).

antipattern: Flattening/counting module parameters (e.g. list comprehension over op.parameters()) inside a per-forward-pass method instead of caching it once at construction time.
match_logic: A comprehension that iterates over a nested .parameters() call (either flattening params or counting them via sum(1 for _ ...)), which is invariant across forward calls and should be hoisted into __init__.
positive_example: params = [param for op in self._basic_ops for param in op.parameters()]

antipattern: A device/context query like cuda::sm_count() (which resolves the current device via driver calls each time) is invoked inside a per-config constructor or lambda body rather than hoisted and computed once outside the loop.
match_logic: Match a call to cuda::sm_count() (or similarly cheap-looking but driver-backed device query such as current_device/sm_count/device_count) occurring inside a constructor body or lambda that is invoked repeatedly per candidate config, where the value does not depend on loop-varying inputs and could be hoisted.
negative_example: const size_t sm_count = static_cast<size_t>(cuda::sm_count());  // hoisted once before the loop
